docs.title: 1.1 Aerodrome introduction
docs.text:
Velana International Airport (VRMM) is the primary airport for the Maldives, located on Hulhulé Island, east of the capital, Male'. The aerodrome first opened in 1960, undergoing several developments and rennovations across the time it has been open, including a new runway which entered full operational service in 2022, and a new international terminal opening in 2025. Velena International handles effectively all of the nations international and domestic traffic, along with being home to the wolds largest seaplane airline and base.

ICAO IATA Location Elevation Magnetic variation Time zone VRMM MLE Hulhulé Island, North Male' (Kaafu) Atoll, Maldives 6ft (1.8m) AMSL ~ 3°W UTC +5

docs.title: 1.6 Prefered runway operation
docs.text:
The runway in operation at Velena international should be decided based off the current weather / wind conditions. Between 36 and 18, the runway with the highest headwind should be used. If headwind in equal (zero), or if the runway can not be decided on, the prefered runway is 36.

The runway(s) in operation at the seaplane base are dependent on the runway in operation at the international airport, as shown in the table below.

International airport Seaplane base (standard) Seaplane base (high crosswind) 36 NL for takeoffNC or SL for landing (based off wind direction) 18 SC for takeoffSL for landing

Useful tip

We recomend controllers to check and use the runway(s) being operated in real life through sites such as FR24, ADSB Exchange, etc. If controllers think that the runway in operation should change, they can check what is being operated in the real world to help their decision.

docs.title: 2.1 Area of responisbility detailed
docs.text:
Callsign Area Of Responsibility VRMM_GND Male ground is responsible for:- Issuing IFR & VFR clearances (as detailed in section 3.)- Controlling the apron area- Issuing pushback clearance (as detailed in section 4.) VRMM_TWR Male tower is responsible for:- The controlling of maneuvering areas- Issuing taxi in / out clearances (as detailed in section 5.)- Issuing takeoff & landing clearances (as detailed in section 6.)- The controlling of all aircraft within the Male TMA

Information

In real life Male Ground only provides IFR / VFR clearances, and pushback clearances. However, if coordinated, it is not a problem for Ground to take over the responsibility of taxing aircraft from Male Tower.

docs.title: 3.1 Clearance details
docs.text:
Pilots may be expected to only request for ATC clearance provided they are ready for pushback.

When calling for clearance, pilots may be expected to provide the following: Callsign, destination, ATIS letter, requested flight level, aircraft type, current location / stand, and any special requests / remarks.

If a pilot provides an incorrect ATIS letter, the controller should correct it and ensure the pilot reads it back.

docs.title: 3.2 IFR Clearances
docs.text:
Before providing an IFR clearance, the handling controller should ensure that the aircraft's flight plan is valid. The controller should notify and recive a readback from the pilot for all meaningful changes mnade to the flight plan.

IFR Clearances should contain the following information: Cleared airway (if applicable), final altitude / flight level, squawk, and cleared SID.

Example IFR clearance

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) cleared to Gan via Z652, FL200, squawk 7433, ATISA1A departure

“Cleared to Gan via Z652, FL200, squawk 7433, ATISA1A departure, Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102)”

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) readback correct, report ready for pushback and startup with the tow truck connected

Warning

Initial climb is NOT provided in an IFR clearance. Instead, it is provided during taxi or takeoff clearance by the applicable controller, as defined in section 5.2.

docs.title: 3.3 VFR Clearances
docs.text:
idk

Example VFR clearance

X

X

docs.title: 4.1 Pushback requirements
docs.text: When pushing back aircraft, controllers should ensure that the pushing aircraft does not impede other pushing or taxiing aircraft. Simultaneous pushbacks should only be approved if there is a minimum spacing of two stands between the two aircraft.

docs.title: 4.2 South GA Apron
docs.text: Aircraft generally do not require pushback. Startup clearance should be issued, with the instruction “Report when ready for taxi”.

docs.title: 4.3 Main Apron
docs.text: Aircraft require pushback from the main apron. Aircraft should be instructed to pushback facing either North or South, generally in the direction facing their departure holding point, or which provides the shortest taxi route, as long as it does not impede taxiing traffic.

docs.title: 4.4 North Apron
docs.text: Aircraft generally do not require pushback. Startup clearance should be issued, with the instruction “Report when ready for taxi”.

docs.title: 4.5 North GA Apron
docs.text: Aircraft generally do not require pushback. Startup clearance should be issued, with the instruction “Report when ready for taxi”.

docs.title: 4.6 East Apron
docs.text: Aircraft require pushback from the east apron. Aircraft should be instructed to pushback facing South.

docs.title: 4.7 Seaplane terminal
docs.text: Aircraft do not require pushback. Startup clearance should be issued with the readback correct message as described in section 1.3.3.

docs.title: 4.8 Phraseology
docs.text:
Example pushback clearance

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) pushback and startup approved facing north

“Pushback and startup approved facing north, Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102)”

Example startup clearance

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) startup approved, report when ready for taxi

“Startup approved, report when ready for taxi, Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102)”

docs.title: 5.1 Standard departure points
docs.text: Runway Medium (from North Apron) Medium (turboprop) Medium and heavy (jet) 36 TWY D4 or D5 TWY D5 TWY D6 or D7 18 TWY D4 or D3 or TWY C Holding Position if entering via TWY D1 or D2 TWY D4 TWY D3 and TWY C Holding Position if entering via TWY D1 or D2

docs.title: 5.2 Taxi instruction
docs.text:
Aircraft should be instructed to taxi to a holding point based on their weight category, as per section 5.1, unless otherwise requested by a pilot. Controllers may ask a pilot whether they have a preferred departure point, or whether they are able to accept an intersection departure. However, it is at the pilot's discretion to accept or decline such a request.

Example taxi instruction

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) taxi to holdingpoint D5 via B4

"Taxi to holdingpoint D5 via B4, Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102)"

docs.title: 5.3 Departure instruction
docs.text:
During taxi or takeoff clearance, the controller should provide pilots with their initial departure instruction. This should include their initial climb, and an instruction to fly via the SID.

Example taxi instruction (with departure instruction)

Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102) departure instruction maintain 6000ft [on / via] [SID / ATISA1A departure], taxi to holdingpoint D5 via B4

"Maintain 6000ft on SID, taxi to holdingpoint D5 via B4, Sky Surfer 102 (DQA102)"
